transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: Good evening everyone. Sprint 4 retrospective. UAT concluded Monday and Tuesday — client signed off on Wednesday. So we're closing the sprint properly today. I sent the retro agenda this morning. Let's go through it. What went well first.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: I'll start. Team resilience this sprint was genuinely impressive. Day 1 the environment was blocked. Day 3 the demo failed in front of the client. Day 4 we found a prod SSL cert issue. Most teams would have fragmented or started pointing fingers. This team diagnosed each problem, communicated clearly, and kept moving. That's not nothing.

transcript.speaker: Varsha
transcript.text: The async queue fix — I'm proud of that. It wasn't in the original sprint scope. We identified it mid-sprint as a critical gap, got buy-in, built it properly with real infrastructure, and it fixed the 2 intermittent prod failures that had been sitting open for a week. That was good technical problem-solving under pressure.

transcript.speaker: Ajay
transcript.text: 34 of 34 tests passing at the end. We started the sprint with 0 tests running because the environment wasn't up. By the end, full coverage, all passing, including edge cases the client threw at us during UAT that weren't in the original scenario doc. That's a real quality result.

transcript.speaker: Suraj
transcript.text: The Thursday session where Varsha, Ajay and I went through the edge cases together — that was effective. We found 4 things in that 2-hour session that would have been UAT failures. Collaborative testing like that should be a sprint ritual, not just something we do under pressure.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: Redis stability. Once infra provisioned it, Redis ran without a single issue for the rest of the sprint. The monitoring alerts I set up fired zero times. Infrastructure, when it's done right, becomes invisible — you don't hear from it. That's what we achieved.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: Client feedback from UAT was better than I expected given how the week started. They specifically commented that the team was responsive and transparent throughout. That matters for the long-term relationship. Okay — now what didn't go well. I want the real version.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: Day 1, Monday April 6. Staging environment was not functional. I had raised the infra ticket the Friday before. The config wasn't applied until Monday 1:20pm. We lost an entire morning on Day 1 of a 10-day sprint. This is the same pattern as Sprint 3 — we raised the issue, infra delayed, team waited. The Sprint 3 retro action item was to get Vivek into pre-sprint planning. That happened. But the infra turnaround time is still a risk.

transcript.speaker: Ajay
transcript.text: Day 3 demo failure. I want to be honest about this one. The fix was deployed to staging-v2. The demo was on staging-v1. Both Rakesh and I knew before the demo which environment we were using — we just didn't cross-check. A 2-minute verification before the demo would have prevented it. We got complacent.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: That's fair. I take responsibility for that. My mistake was assuming without verifying. We now have a single deployment pipeline so both environments are always in sync — that's the fix. But the culture of 'verify before demo' needs to be a checklist item, not an assumption.

transcript.speaker: Varsha
transcript.text: The SSL cert issue — I want to name this directly. Vivek flagged the prod-staging cert mismatch in an infra sync meeting 2 weeks before the sprint. It was noted and not acted on. By anyone, including me — I was in that meeting. We let a known risk sit unresolved and it hit us on Day 4. That's a process failure across the team, not just infra.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: Yes. I raised it but I also didn't follow up loudly enough. I mentioned it once and moved on. Next time I flag a critical infra risk, I need to keep it visible — repeat it in standups, add it to the risk register, not just mention it once in a sync.

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: Scope changes mid-sprint — this was a real problem. Sprint 4 had the original payment API scope. Then mid-sprint client added the expectation that the async retry queue would also be done. Then the idempotency check came up during UAT prep. Sprint 4 expanded by about 30% in scope compared to what we planned on Day 1. Each addition was reasonable individually, but together they created pressure we didn't plan for.

transcript.speaker: Suraj
transcript.text: And the scope additions came in informally — a Slack message here, a client call there. They didn't go through a formal change process. So the team absorbed them without always realising how much the total scope had grown.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: Agreed. That's a process we need to fix. Anything that adds to the sprint backlog mid-sprint should go through a formal decision — is it in scope for this sprint, or does it go to the next sprint backlog? Not informal absorb-and-adjust.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: Good points. Now action items — what are we committing to for Sprint 5? Real commitments, not aspirations.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: Mandatory environment parity checklist before sprint starts. Before Day 1 standup, I run a comparison between staging and prod — SSL certs, network config, DB config, environment variables. If anything doesn't match, the sprint does not start until it's resolved. Written checklist, signed off by me and Ajay.

transcript.speaker: Ajay
transcript.text: I'll co-own that checklist with Vivek. And from my side — pre-demo verification steps become a mandatory 5-minute checklist before any client-facing demo. Environment, deployment version, smoke test result. No exceptions.

transcript.speaker: Varsha
transcript.text: SSL cert rotation calendar. We set up a shared calendar with all cert expiry dates and a 4-week renewal reminder. Vivek and I both have visibility. No more finding out about certs when something breaks.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: I'll set that up over the weekend. It's a 30-minute task.

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: Scope freeze. Client scope is locked 3 days before sprint start. After that, any addition goes to the following sprint backlog unless it's a critical fix with explicit approval from Vinod sir. That's the rule.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: I'll communicate that directly to the client. They need to hear it from me, not just see it in a process document.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: I want to add one more. Risk register as a living document. Any technical risk flagged — by anyone — goes into the risk register immediately with owner and review date. Not just mentioned in a meeting. Vivek's SSL flag 2 weeks ago should have been in a risk register with a resolution target date. If it had been, someone would have followed up.

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: I'll create the risk register template this weekend and share it Monday morning. Anyone can add to it. I'll review it every Wednesday in the weekly call.

transcript.speaker: Suraj
transcript.text: One last thing — the collaborative edge-case session Varsha, Ajay and I did on Thursday. Can we make that a scheduled event at the midpoint of every sprint? Two hours, backend plus frontend plus QA, go through the edge cases together. It was genuinely the most productive 2 hours of the sprint.

transcript.speaker: Ajay
transcript.text: Absolutely. Add it to the sprint ceremonies. Sprint day 5, cross-functional edge case review, 2 hours.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: I'll close with this. Sprint 4 was the hardest sprint on this project. It had more things go wrong than any sprint before it. But the output — 34 tests passing, async queue built, UAT signed off, client relationship intact — is better than we could have expected after how Monday looked. That tells me this team has matured significantly. I'm confident in Sprint 5. Let's go into it with these lessons in hand. Good work everyone.
